hero_tagline: A self-hostable memory layer for the anime and TV you've already watched. Kyomiru surfaces shows that quietly got new episodes while you were away.
features_subheading: Track once. Get notified when it's back.
feature_1_body: Finished shows automatically surface in New Content the moment a new episode airs — no manual checking.
feature_2_body: Every show you've ever watched in one searchable grid, grouped by status: in progress, new, watched.
feature_3_body: A drag-and-drop prioritised watchlist separate from your finished shelf. Always know what's next.
feature_4_body: Titles and descriptions served in your preferred locale — English, Japanese, Spanish, or French.
feature_5_body: TV imports get promoted to anime when TMDb + AniList agree. Per-user override always available.
feature_6_body: Add Kyomiru to your home screen. Your library stays browsable even when offline.
how_subheading: One extension captures your history. One server remembers.
how_step1_body: Browse Crunchyroll or Netflix normally. The Kyomiru Chrome extension notices your session — no passwords stored.
how_step2_body: Daily or on-demand, the extension paginates your provider history and streams normalised chunks to your Kyomiru instance.
how_step3_body: Shows are matched to TMDb and AniList in the background. Titles, seasons, and episodes are resolved in your language.
how_step4_body: Shows that aired new episodes while you were away move into New Content. Everything else stays tidy in your library.
how_footnote: Provider credentials stay in your browser. Kyomiru servers never talk to Crunchyroll or Netflix directly — that's the whole point.
providers_subheading: Kyomiru is provider-pluggable — more coming soon.
providers_disclaimer: Kyomiru is not affiliated with Crunchyroll or Netflix. All trademarks belong to their respective owners.
install_subheading: Join the hosted beta or spin up your own instance.
install_hosted_body: Kyomiru's hosted instance is invite-only while we harden the sync pipeline. Request access on GitHub — or skip the queue entirely and self-host.
install_selfhost_body: Open source under AGPL-3.0. Clone the repo, fill in .env, and run docker compose up. Multi-arch images on Quay.io. Helm chart available for Kubernetes.
install_ext_body: The extension is how your watch history gets in. Grab the latest .zip from GitHub Releases and load it unpacked.
install_ext_hint: Not on the Chrome Web Store yet — load via chrome://extensions → Developer Mode → Load unpacked.
faq_subheading: Still curious? Open an issue on GitHub.
faq_a1: Yes. Kyomiru is fully open source under AGPL-3.0. The hosted beta is free during the invite period. If you self-host, you pay only for whatever VPS or home server you run it on — a Raspberry Pi or small cloud VM is enough.
faq_q2: Why is sign-up invite-only right now?
faq_a2: The hosted instance is still in early access. We're letting a small group in while we harden the sync pipeline and manage hosting costs. Self-hosting has no access gate — clone the repo and you're good to go.
faq_a3: Crunchyroll and Netflix today. The Chrome extension is built around a ProviderAdapter interface, so more can be added without touching the server. Open a GitHub issue to suggest the next one.
faq_q4: Does Kyomiru store my Crunchyroll or Netflix credentials?
faq_a4: No. The Chrome extension reads your existing in-browser session — it never sees your password or stores your login credentials. For Crunchyroll, the extension captures a short-lived Bearer JWT from outgoing requests; for Netflix, it uses your active browser cookies. Neither credential is ever sent to Kyomiru's servers. The servers never call Crunchyroll or Netflix directly.
faq_a5: The extension is built for Chrome's Manifest V3 platform. The provider adapter logic is framework-agnostic, so a Firefox port is technically straightforward — it just hasn't been built yet. If that's important to you, open an issue.
faq_q6: Why isn't the extension on the Chrome Web Store?
faq_a6: It requests broad host permissions on streaming sites in order to read watch history, which triggers a manual review process. We chose to ship via GitHub Releases while the API is still evolving. You load it unpacked through chrome://extensions → Developer Mode — it's the same code, just not store-distributed yet.
faq_q7: How much does it cost to self-host?
faq_a7: Kyomiru runs as two Node.js services (API + web) plus Postgres and Redis. A $5-10/month VPS or a spare Raspberry Pi 4 is sufficient for a single household. Multi-arch Docker images (amd64 + arm64) are published to Quay.io on every release.
faq_q8: Does this landing page track me?
faq_a8: No. There are no third-party analytics scripts, no cookies, no tracking pixels, and no fingerprinting. The only external resource loaded is the Inter font from Google Fonts on page load.
footer_copyright: © {{year}} Kyomiru contributors. Open source under AGPL-3.0.
footer_trademark: Not affiliated with Crunchyroll or Netflix. All trademarks belong to their respective owners.
meta_title: Kyomiru — Your anime & TV watch memory
meta_description: A self-hostable memory layer for anime and TV. Kyomiru surfaces shows that quietly got new episodes while you were away.
